Here's another one. Very often we want to present our idea to somebody, right? And one of the things we learn with the actual tactics of the straight line is we ask for permission to tell someone about our product or idea or whatever we're offering. We don't just start pitching them and say, here's what it is. We say to them, listen, you know, if you have 60 seconds, I'd like to share an idea with you. You got a minute? Now, notice my turn. I say, if you have 60 seconds, I want to share an idea. Got a minute? You have a minute? I don't say to you, got a minute? Ha ha. I say, watch. Now watch the difference. Say, if you have 60 seconds, I'd like to share an idea with you. Got a minute? That's called the reasonable man tone. I'm just, you know, got a minute? You know, you're reasonable, I'm reasonable. Anybody's got a minute. Watch this. If you say to someone, if you have 60 seconds, I want to share an idea with you. You got a minute? 99% of people say, well, yeah, sure, I got a minute because everybody's got a minute. If you say it like, if you have 60 seconds, I want to share an idea with you. You got a minute? Your response rate drops by half because one way you are the reasonable, kind, helping person. At this point, you've already established that you care, that you really want to know about their health and well-being, whatever it might be, right? So you're already in a bit of rapport and then you're giving that reasonable man tone saying, you know, you got a minute and bam, they're like, yeah, sure, why not? He's a nice guy. He cares. He's just like me and bam, you're in. And an example of the reasonable man would be where you say, so when, you know, on your closing, let's say you get into, they're almost there and you say, you know, give me a shot. You know, you're going to be very, very impressed. And you say, sound fair enough? Now look at my hands. Sound fair enough? My body language for reasonable, let's just say, put my hands up. Sound. And I say, sound fair enough? And the implied words that come after that is I'm reasonable. You're reasonable. It's a reasonable thing, right? I don't have to say I'm reasonable. You, without the tonality, I have to say, listen, I'm reasonable. You're reasonable. Come on. Just do it. But I don't have to say it. Instead of saying, sound fair enough? And your voice saying, well, he's being reasonable. I want to be reasonable. I've always thought it's good to be reasonable. And that's the unconscious communication. You start to actually affect someone else's dialogue. That's your reasonable man tonality.
